sound_type: Tactical silence then burst. Minimal vocalization during mammal hunt approach. No discrete calls. No whistles. Reduced echolocation. Silence is adaptive (marine mammal prey acoustically sensitive). Post-kill: vocalizations resume. E=8 through restraint.
ecosystem: Inland water -- Puget Sound, Hood Canal, Strait of Juan de Fuca, San Juan Islands. Bigg's orcas range throughout coastal Pacific from Alaska to California, with regular presence in Puget Sound following pinniped distribution.
season: Resident (year-round range; opportunistic, following pinniped abundance rather than salmon seasonality).

coordinates.description: Reference for Hood Canal / central Puget Sound -- key Bigg's orca hunting area for harbor seals and Steller sea lions. Also present throughout San Juan Islands and Strait of Juan de Fuca.

taxonomy_note.description: Bigg's killer whale (also: transient killer whale). Orcinus orca, Family Delphinidae. Named for Michael Bigg (1939-1990), Canadian biologist who pioneered photo-identification for cetaceans and revealed orca ecotype structure. Bigg's orcas are genetically distinct from Southern Residents (~100,000+ years divergence, no interbreeding). Mammal-eating ecotype: harbor seals, Steller sea lions, harbor porpoises, Dall's porpoises, minke whales. Acoustic silence during mammal hunt (adaptive: mammal prey acoustically sensitive). Cultural transmission: calves learn mammal-eating from mother. Not endangered; recovering as pinniped populations recover under MMPA 1972. Population estimate: ~250-350 Northeast Pacific individuals.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Orcinus orca: see degrees 270-271. 'Bigg's' honors Michael Bigg (1939-1990). 'Transient': original designation reflecting more peripatetic ranging compared to site-faithful residents.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Same genus as Southern Residents (Orcinus orca). The ecotype distinction is behavioral and cultural, not (yet) taxonomic. Some researchers argue Bigg's and Southern Residents should be separate species; current taxonomy treats them as ecotypes of one species.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Cetacea, Odontoceti. Third consecutive entry. Three consecutive Delphinidae: pod call (270), whistles/clicks (271), hunt sequence (272).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Third consecutive Delphinidae. The orca arc (270-276) is creating the most family-concentrated run in the SPS engine.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: HUNT SILENCE: near-zero acoustic output. POST-KILL: same frequency range as resident orca (discrete calls 500-20,000 Hz, whistles 1,000-18,000 Hz, clicks 10,000-80,000+ Hz). The hunt is characterized by the ABSENCE of sound.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: During hunt approach: no significant acoustic energy. During burst/post-kill: broadband.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Hunt sequence defined by silence. The acoustic event is the absence of the usual cetacean soundscape. Post-kill vocalizations resemble resident orca calls in frequency range but are Bigg's-specific in structure.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Silence phase: minutes to hours. Burst: seconds to minutes of high-activity pursuit. Post-kill: variable vocalization bout.
song_metrics.note_structure: HUNT SILENCE: No note structure -- acoustic absence. BURST: physical sounds (breaching, tail-slapping, high-speed hydrodynamic noise). POST-KILL: Bigg's-specific vocalizations, variable.
song_metrics.note_types: The defining note type is SILENCE. Post-kill: variable calls, less stereotyped than SRKW discrete calls. Bigg's orca post-kill vocalizations are documented but less systematically studied than SRKW calls.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: The hunt sequence has a temporal structure defined by silence, not by note patterns: extended silence → burst (acoustic + physical events) → post-kill vocalizations.
song_metrics.timbre: During hunt: nothing. During burst: hydrodynamic, percussive (breaching, physical contact). Post-kill: vocal, cetacean-typical.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=8 -- Maximum through restraint. The silence contains maximum energy. The burst releases it.
song_metrics.tempo: Silence phase: zero acoustic tempo. Burst: maximum physical speed (30+ km/h). Click rate during any echolocation: variable, conservative during approach.
song_metrics.context: PREDATORY HUNT. Silence is adaptive: marine mammal prey are acoustically sensitive. Harbor seals detect predator sounds and flee. Bigg's orca acoustic silence during approach prevents detection. The silence is purposeful restraint, not acoustic incapacity.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Bigg's orcas throughout Northeast Pacific use acoustic silence during mammal hunts. The strategy is consistent across the range -- further evidence that it is culturally transmitted.
song_metrics.key_property: TACTICAL SILENCE THEN BURST. ORCA ARC (3 OF 7). Bigg's / transient killer whale -- named for Michael Bigg. Mammal-eating, acoustically silent during hunting. Maximum ecotype contrast with Southern Resident (salmon-eating, continuously vocal). Same genus, same waters, 100,000+ years no interbreeding. Cultural divergence without genetic exchange. E=8 through restraint. Harbor seals, Steller sea lions, porpoises. Puget Sound, Hood Canal, Strait of Juan de Fuca.

plumage.overall: Same species as Southern Residents: black and white countershading, white eye patch. Bigg's orca saddle patches tend to be 'closed' (darker, more opaque) vs. SRKW 'open' saddle patches -- a field identification characteristic. Individual identification possible by photo-ID.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Closed saddle patch pattern (compared to open/lighter SRKW saddle patches). At-sea identification of ecotype requires experience and often comparison.

physical_description.overall: See degrees 270-271. Adult males: 6-8m, 3,600-5,400 kg. Adult females: 5-7m, 1,400-3,600 kg. Closed saddle patches distinguish Bigg's from SRKW in the field.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Closed saddle patches. Group behavior: traveling in silence through seal haul-out areas is a Bigg's behavioral signature.
physical_description.field_id: Large black and white cetacean in closed saddle patch configuration, traveling silently through pinniped habitat. The silence itself is a field identification cue.

nodes.title: Tactical Silence -- E=8 Through Restraint
nodes.description: Acoustic silence during mammal hunt approach. Adaptive: marine mammal prey acoustically sensitive. The restraint is the strategy. Maximum energy held back in the silence; released in the burst.

nodes.title: The Burst -- Maximum Commitment
nodes.description: 30+ km/h pursuit. Coordinated group action. Breach, spy-hop, physical contact. Silence breaks at the moment of maximum commitment. The silence was always preparation for this moment.

nodes.title: Ecotype Contrast -- Cultural Divergence
nodes.description: Bigg's (mammal, silent) vs. Southern Resident (salmon, vocal). Same genus, same waters, 100,000+ years no interbreeding. Divergence maintained by cultural transmission. The most dramatic ecotype contrast in animal biology.

nodes.title: Named for Michael Bigg -- Photo-ID Pioneer
nodes.description: Michael Bigg (1939-1990): inventor of cetacean photo-identification, revealed ecotype structure. Same technique used by Balcomb for Southern Residents. Bigg's killer whale named in his honor posthumously.

cross_references.species: Puget Sound Orca S01 Pod Call
cross_references.relationship: ECOTYPE CONTRAST. SRKW continuously vocal (discrete calls). Bigg's acoustically silent (hunt silence). Same genus, opposite acoustic strategies. Contrast makes both entries richer.
ECOTYPE CONTRAST. SRKW uses continuous echolocation during foraging. Bigg's minimizes echolocation during approach to avoid alerting mammal prey. Same sensory system, opposite deployment strategy.
